perturbation.original_text: Each Party acknowledges that it is aware (and that its affiliates, employees, representatives and consultants who are apprised of the possible Transaction have been advised) that the United States and other applicable securities laws prohibit any person who is aware of material, non-public information about a company obtained directly or indirectly from that company, from purchasing or selling securities of such company or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party acknowledges that it is aware that purchasing or selling securities based on confidential information may have risks.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit acknowledgement of insider trading prohibitions weakens the legally required disclosure about securities laws. The altered text now does not explicitly state that using material non-public information is prohibited under securities laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text omits the clear prohibition against insider trading as mandated by Rule 10b-5, which makes it unlawful to use any manipulative or deceptive device in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. By only mentioning 'risks', it does not adequately inform the parties of their obligations under securities law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the original contract's explicit acknowledgement of insider trading prohibitions, creating a legal contradiction. The original text specifically mentioned the prohibition against using material, non-public information, while the changed text only refers to 'risks' associated with trading on confidential information. This omission directly contradicts Rule 10b-5, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which prohibits 'any manipulative or deceptive device' in the purchase or sale of securities and also prohibits omissions of material facts that would be misleading. The altered text fails to adequately inform the parties of their obligations under securities laws, moving from a specific prohibition to a vague warning.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall use the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information only for the purpose of evaluating the Transaction and for the purposes of the ongoing Transaction, and shall protect such Confidential Information from disclosure to third parties, using the same degree of care used to protect its own proprietary information of like importance, but in any case using no less than a reasonable degree of care.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall use the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information only for the purpose of evaluating the Transaction and for the purposes of the ongoing Transaction.
perturbation.explanation: The omission removes the requirement to protect the Confidential Information from disclosure to third parties. This introduces a legal contradiction by neglecting to provide reasonable safeguards for personal information as required under privacy laws, such as those related to data breach notification. This is especially relevant if the confidential information includes Personally Identifiable Information (PII).
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text fails to meet the requirements of the New York SHIELD Act, which mandates reasonable security measures to protect private information. By removing the obligation to protect confidential information from disclosure, the agreement exposes individuals to potential harm from data breaches, directly contradicting the law's purpose.

perturbation.justification: The omission of the clause requiring the Receiving Party to protect Confidential Information from disclosure to third parties creates a legal contradiction. The original text ensured a 'reasonable degree of care' in protecting sensitive data, which aligns with the intent of data security laws. The changed text removes this protection, potentially violating laws like the New York SHIELD Act, which mandates reasonable security measures for private information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 'Data security protections' under NYS General Business Article 39-F, indicating a legal expectation for such protections, which the modified contract fails to provide. The omission directly weakens the obligation to safeguard confidential information, leading to a clear conflict with data protection principles.

perturbation.original_text: Without prejudice to the rights and remedies otherwise available to the Parties, the Parties agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by either Party or the affiliates, employees, representatives and/or consultants of either Party and, accordingly, that the Parties shall be entitled to equitable relief, including injunctive relief and/or specific performance, if either Party (including the affiliates, employees, representatives and/or consultants thereof) breaches or threatens to breach any of the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Without prejudice to the rights and remedies otherwise available to the Parties, the Parties agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by either Party or the affiliates, employees, representatives and/or consultants of either Party.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of the right to equitable relief, including injunctive relief and/or specific performance, limits the remedies available to the parties in case of a breach. This could be problematic under contract law where specific performance is a typical remedy when monetary damages are insufficient, such as cases involving unique assets. This particularly contradicts general principles of contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The omission of the right to equitable relief contradicts UCC § 2-716, which allows for specific performance when goods are unique or in other proper circumstances. By removing the availability of equitable relief, the agreement limits the remedies available to the non-breaching party in situations where monetary damages are inadequate, which undermines established principles of contract law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: , from early projects like the restatement of basic legal subjects to a collaboration with NCCUSL in developing and monitoring the Uniform Commercial Code. View more information about the ALI collections The National Bankruptcy Archives (NBA) In 2000, the Biddle Law Library and the American College of Bankruptcy collaborated to establish the National Bankruptcy Archives (NBA), a national repository of materials relating to the history of debtor-creditor relations, bankruptcy, and the reorganization of debt. The NBA currently contains over 1,000 linear feet of personal papers and institutional records. View more information about the NBA collections Explore the NBA Oral History collections Biddle Collections The Collections consist of manuscript collections, rare books, oral histories, and a select number of Penn Carey Law School’s records. The manuscript collections include the papers of George Wharton Pepper, Bernie Segal, William Draper Lewis, and David L. Bazelon. The Raymond F. Trent Collection consists of books, periodicals, articles, and audio tapes concerning the history of education and practice of Black lawyers in the United States. Some records relating to the operations of Penn Carey Law School are maintained by the University Archives . Please contact us for more information. View more information about the Biddle Law Library Collections Explore the collection of The Report the Law School’s yearbook Explore the Penn Carey Law Oral History Project collection Contact & Hours The Archives and Special Collections office is located on the fourth floor of Tanenbaum Hall. The mailing address is: University of Pennsylvania Carey Law School Biddle Law Library - Archives Department 3501 Sansom Street Philadelphia, PA 19104-3406 For questions about the Archives, the Rare Books Collection, or other special collections, please email the Archives Department at biddlearchives@law.upenn.edu . The Archives is open Tuesday-Friday, 10AM to 4PM. Appointments should be made 2 weeks in advance of the visit. Visitors will be asked to show identification. Policies Registration All researchers are asked to fill out our Researcher Registration Form. This form can be filled out prior to your visit or on the day of your arrival. Archives - Researcher Registration Form On-site Research Items in the Archives do not circulate. If you wish to come to the Archives to conduct research, you may do so free of charge. However, you must contact the department ahead of time to schedule an appointment. Appointments should be made 2 weeks in advance of desired research date. Visitors to the Law School will be asked to show identification. Remote Research For patrons who cannot travel to the Law School to conduct research, the Archives will assist in the identification of potentially relevant materials for your research topic or for photocopying. We do not transcribe, paraphrase, or otherwise interpret information for the researcher. Research requests generally take 2-3 weeks to complete, depending on the nature of the question and the number of outstanding requests at the time of submission. In addition, the Archives can only offer one hour of research assistance, patrons are asked to make an appointment for requests that will take longer than one hour to complete. Please also note that remote research requests are limited, and the Archives can only handle copying/scanning requests up to 20 individual documents. If a request exceeds this number, we suggest you schedule an appointment to visit the Archives in person. Handling Materials Wash hands before touching materials. Protective gloves may be required. Remove only one folder from a box at a time. Documents should be maintained in the exact order they are arranged in each file. If a document is out of order, notify the Archives staff. Make no marks or erasures on any document. Use strips provided to mark pages for copying. Eating or drinking is not permitted. Use pencils only, no pens or markers. Laptop
perturbation.justification: The original text provides for equitable relief, including injunctive relief and specific performance, in the event of a breach. The changed text removes the availability of equitable relief. The Law Explanation states this contradicts UCC § 2-716, which allows for specific performance when goods are unique. Although no UCC § 2-716 snippet was provided, the Perturbation Explanation notes that the omission undermines established principles of contract law where specific performance is a typical remedy when monetary damages are insufficient. Therefore, removing the possibility of specific performance creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees that, for a period of one (1) year from the Effective Date hereof, neither it nor any of its affiliates will solicit for employment, directly or indirectly, any member of the other Party's senior management with whom it first had contact, or who was specifically identified to it, during the period of its evaluation of the Transaction; provided, however, that this paragraph will not prevent either Party from -------- ------- employing any person who contacts such Party on his or her own initiative without any direct or indirect solicitation by or encouragement from such Party. For purposes of this paragraph, "solicit for employment" shall not be deemed to include any general solicitations of employment by one Party not specifically directed towards employees of the other Party. Nothing herein shall prevent either Party from, directly or indirectly, soliciting for employment or hiring any member of the other Party's senior management whose employment with such other Party has been terminated.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees that, for a period of one (1) year from the Effective Date hereof, neither it nor any of its affiliates will solicit for employment, directly or indirectly, any member of the other Party's senior management with whom it first had contact, or who was specifically identified to it, during the period of its evaluation of the Transaction.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of the safe harbor clauses regarding unsolicited applications and terminated employees effectively broadens the non-solicitation agreement. This could be seen as an unreasonable restraint of trade, especially in states with strong protections against non-compete agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The removal of the exceptions to the non-solicitation agreement makes it potentially overbroad and thus an unlawful restraint of trade under California law, should this agreement be applied to someone residing in California.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text removes crucial exceptions to the non-solicitation agreement, potentially creating an overbroad restraint of trade. California Business and Professions Code Section 16600(a), as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, explicitly voids "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind." The removal of the safe harbor clauses, as explained in the Perturbation and Law Explanations, broadens the scope of the non-solicitation agreement, potentially violating this statute if the agreement is applied to someone residing in California. The omission significantly impacts the enforceability of the contract by potentially violating California law.
